title: Question 1
statement_text: Assuming only that each $a_i \neq 0$, what geometric constraints can be imposed on a set $S \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ which imply non-trivial uniform upper bounds on $\Pr(X \in S)$?
context_text: The paper introduces a new generalisation of the Littlewood–Offord problem to $\mathbb{R}^d$ in which no assumption is made on the sizes of the nonzero vectors $a_1,\ldots,a_n$, only that they are nonzero. The authors observe that this makes the question much more about the geometry of $S$ than about spatial anti-concentration of $X = a_1\xi_1 + \cdots + a_n\xi_n$, giving it a very different flavour from previous Littlewood–Offord variants.

title: Conjecture 1.2
statement_text: In Theorem 1.1 we have $\Pr(X \in S) \leq C_S/\sqrt{n}$, for some $C_S$ depending only on $S$.
context_text: Theorem 1.1 establishes $\Pr(X \in S) \leq n^{-1/2+\alpha}$ for any $\alpha > 0$ when $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ is definable with respect to an o-minimal structure and contains no line segment; the conjecture posits that the optimal $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ bound from the classical Erd\H{o}s–Littlewood–Offord theorem also holds in this geometric setting. The authors prove it when $S$ satisfies the generic intersection property, which covers convex or irreducible algebraic plane curves in $\mathbb{R}^2$, irreducible complex algebraic curves, and the boundary of a 3-dimensional ball. They also remark that a full resolution of the Gotsman–Linial conjecture would imply Conjecture 1.2 for all semi-algebraic sets not containing a line segment.
